questions.text: I often sense what someone really means even when their words say something different.
I feel most confident when I'm free to improvise and respond in real time.
I believe that how you do anything is how you do everything, so I attend to details.
I would rather make a bold, imperfect decision than let the group stall.
I hold the view that the moral demand of suffering is not merely a feeling but a genuine perception of ethical reality.
I find that the thought of others benefiting from my effort is the strongest motivation I know.
I focus on the explicit agenda of a meeting rather than the interpersonal dynamics.
I create project timelines with specific milestones and check-in points.
I believe that the moral significance of suffering is what gives ethical reasoning its urgency and its necessity.
I let the group find its own course, even if I see a different path.
I find that I can sit with uncomfortable news without being destabilized by it.
I am comfortable telling people directly what I need from them.
I believe that calm, measured analysis of a painful situation is more likely to lead to an effective and fair response than emotional involvement.
I believe that being needed by others is one of the most important things in life.
I am highly attuned to when an apology is genuine versus perfunctory.
I notice that my pulse quickens noticeably in response to emotional stimuli.
I find that when I invest in myself, everything else in my life improves.
I hold the view that my emotional reactivity is simply the volume setting I was born with.
